7. Details on fire suppression, smoke or fire 
detection, thermal management, ventilation, 
exhaust and deflagration venting systems, if 
provided. 
8. Support arrangement associated with the 
installation, including any required seismic 
restraint. 
9. A commissioning plan complying with 
Section 1207.2.1. 
10. A decommissioning plan complying with 
Section 1207.2.3.

❖ Extensive documentation is required to obtain 
a permit. This helps the Fire official understand 
the extent and location of the hazard and to 
better be able to determine compliance with 
Section 1207. This information is also 
necessary for planning purposes. These 
documents 
specifically 
require 
both 
a 
commissioning and decommissioning plan, 
which is critical to overall safety. ESS must be 
properly installed and tested but also cannot 
simply be abandoned because of the energy 
and hazards that may remain. 
1207.1.4 
Hazard mitigation analysis. A 
failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA) or other 
approved hazard mitigation analysis shall be 
provided in accordance with Section 104.8.2 under 
any of the following conditions: 
1. Where ESS technologies not specifically 
identified in Table 1207.1.1 are provided. 
2. More than one ESS technology is provided in 
a room or enclosed area where there is a 
potential for adverse interaction between 
technologies. 
3. Where allowed as a basis for increasing 
maximum allowable quantities. See Section 
1207.5.2.

❖ This section requires a hazard mitigation 
analysis in the form of a failure modes and 
effects analysis (FMEA) for certain situations. 
This specifically references Section 104.8.2 for 
proper 
analysis 
and 
documentation 
requirements. 
Item 
1 
addresses 
new 
technologies. This allows for flexibility for 
innovative 
technology 
with 
appropriate 
analysis and documentation. 
 
ESS applications are being used in very 
different ways than in the past, and in some 
cases, multiple types of systems may be 
located in a single area within a building. Item 
2 requires this situation to be addressed 
through a hazard analysis to make sure that 
adverse 
conditions 
caused 
by 
mixing 
technologies are not realized. 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1017 
 
 
In terms of Item 3, in some cases the capacity 
of the system surpasses that of Section 
1207.5.2. To apply the requirements of Section 
1207 to these larger systems, a hazard 
mitigation analysis is necessary. 
1207.1.4.1 
Fault 
condition. 
The 
hazard 
mitigation analysis shall evaluate the consequences 
of the following failure modes. Only single failure 
modes shall be considered. 
1. A thermal runaway condition in a single ESS 
rack, module or unit. 
2. Failure of any battery (energy) management 
system. 
3. Failure of any required ventilation or exhaust 
system. 
4. Voltage surges on the primary electric 
supply. 
5. Short circuits on the load side of the ESS. 
6. Failure of the smoke detection, fire detection, 
fire suppression or gas detection system. 
7. Required spill neutralization not being 
provided or failure of a required secondary 
containment system. 
❖ 
This section sets the criteria as to what failure 
modes need to be evaluated for the analysis 
required in Section 1207.1.4. The provisions 
are only asking that a single failure be 
addressed for each scenario, similar to the fact 
that the building and fire codes have been 
written with the concept of a single fire start as 
a reasonable level of design. 
1207.1.4.2 
Analysis approval. The Fire 
official is authorized to approve the hazardous 
mitigation analysis provided that the consequences 
of the hazard mitigation analysis demonstrate: 
1. Fires will be contained within unoccupied 
ESS rooms or areas for the minimum 
duration 
of 
the 
fire-resistance-rated 
separations identified in Section 1207.7.4. 
2. Fires in occupied work centers will be 
detected in time to allow occupants within 
the room or area to safely evacuate. 
3. Toxic and highly toxic gases released during 
fires will not reach concentrations in excess 
of the IDLH level in the building or adjacent 
means of egress routes during the time 
deemed necessary to evacuate occupants 
from any affected area. 
4. 4.Flammable gases released from ESS during 
charging, discharging and normal operation 
will not exceed 25 percent of their lower 
flammability limit (LFL). 
5. 5.Flammable gases released from ESS during 
fire, overcharging and other abnormal 
conditions will be controlled through the use 
of ventilation of the gases, preventing 
accumulation, or by deflagration venting. 
❖ 
A hazardous mitigation analysis is required 
under certain circumstances, identified in 
Section 1207.1.4, to address uncertainties 
associated 
with 
new 
technologies 
and 
configurations. This section describes the 
acceptance criteria to judge acceptability of the 
ESS, whereas Section 1207.1.4.1 identifies 
which failures are to be analyzed. 
1207.1.4.3 
Additional protection measures. 
Construction, equipment and systems that are 
required for the ESS to comply with the hazardous 
mitigation analysis, including but not limited to 
those specifically described in Section 1207, shall 
be installed, maintained and tested in accordance 
with nationally recognized standards and specified 
design parameters. 
❖ 
Essentially, this section ensures that the ESS 
are designed and installed in accordance with 
industry practice and work with the design 
parameters 
approved 
for 
the 
unique 
installation. 
1207.1.5 
Large-scale fire test. Where 
required elsewhere in Section 1207, large-scale fire 
testing shall be conducted on a representative ESS 
in accordance with UL 9540A. The testing shall be 
conducted or witnessed and reported by an 
approved testing laboratory and show that a fire 
involving one ESS will not propagate to an adjacent 
ESS, and where installed within buildings, enclosed 
areas and walk-in units will be contained within the 
room, enclosed area or walk-in unit for a duration 
equal to the fire-resistance rating of the room 
separation specified in Section 1207.7.4. The test 
report shall be provided to the Fire official for 
review and approval in accordance with Section 
104.8.2. 
❖ 
This section provides the details required if 
testing of new and innovative approaches to 
ESS are used. This is based on large-scale fire 
and fault condition testing with specific criteria 
for success and failure. The UL 9540A Test 
Method was specifically developed to cover 
this testing. Since extinguishment of a fire 
involving a thermal runaway in an ESS is 
extremely difficult, if not impossible, the code 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1018 
 
requirements are based on the concept of an 
ESS unit being completely consumed by fire 
without propagating to adjacent ESS. A large-
scale fire test is essential to documenting that 
unit-to-unit propagation will not occur as well as 
generating additional data for necessary risk 
assessments for varied installation locations 
and arrangements relative to exposures. 
1207.1.6 
Fire remediation. Where a fire or 
other event has damaged the ESS and ignition or re-
ignition of the ESS is possible, the system owner, 
agent or lessee shall take the following actions, at 
their expense, to mitigate the hazard or remove 
damaged equipment from the premises to a safe 
location. 
❖ 
This section addresses fire events involving 
lithium-ion battery systems, since lithium-ion 
battery fires have the potential to re-ignite 
hours or even days after initial extinguishment 
by the fire department , which cannot remain 
on the scene indefinitely until the fire-damaged 
ESS is safely removed from the premises. The 
fire remediation requirements, similar to fire 
watch 
requirements, 
make 
the 
owner 
responsible for sending mitigation personnel to 
the scene to take over the remediation 
process. 
1207.1.6.1 
Fire 
mitigation 
personnel. 
Where, in the opinion of the Fire official, it is 
essential for public safety that trained personnel be 
on-site to respond to possible ignition or re-ignition 
of a damaged ESS, the system owner, agent or 
lessee shall immediately dispatch one or more fire 
mitigation personnel to the premise, as required and 
approved, at their expense. These personnel shall 
remain on duty continuously after the fire 
department  leaves the premise until the damaged 
energy storage equipment is removed from the 
premises, or earlier if the Fire official indicates the 
public safety hazard has been abated. 
❖ 
This section sets up the personnel in advance 
so that if fire mitigation is required beyond the 
initial event, there are qualified personnel 
ready to be dispatched. It is important that the 
assigned personnel have the requisite level of 
knowledge 
to 
recognize 
and 
effectively 
respond to the specific hazards of the 
damaged ESS. This section clarifies who is 
responsible for providing this service. It also 
sets out that the owner is financially 
responsible for such services. 
1207.1.6.2 
Duties. On-duty fire mitigation 
personnel shall have the following responsibilities: 
1. Keep a diligent watch for fires, obstructions 
to means of egress and other hazards. 
2. Immediately contact the fire department  if 
their assistance is needed to mitigate any 
hazards or extinguish fires. 
3. Take prompt measures for remediation of 
hazards 
in 
accordance 
with 
the 
decommissioning plan per Section 1207.2.3. 
4. Take prompt measures to assist in the 
evacuation of the public from the structures. 
❖ 
This section simply describes the specific 
responsibilities 
of 
such 
fire 
mitigation 
personnel. This is similar to Section 403.11.1.2 
for fire watch personnel. 
1207.2 Commissioning, 
decommissioning, 
operation and maintenance. Commissioning, 
decommissioning, operation and maintenance shall 
be conducted in accordance with this section. 
❖ 
This 
section 
covers 
commissioning, 
decommissioning, maintenance and testing 
requirements, 
which 
are 
important 
considerations 
for 
providing 
a 
safe, 
codecompliant 
installation 
and 
long-term 
successful performance. 
1207.2.1 
Commissioning. Commissioning 
of newly installed ESS and existing ESS that have 
been 
retrofitted, 
replaced 
or 
previously 
decommissioned and are returning to service shall 
be conducted prior to the ESS being placed in 
service in accordance with a commissioning plan 
that has been approved prior to initiating 
commissioning. The commissioning plan shall 
include the following: 
1. A narrative description of the activities that 
will be accomplished during each phase of 
commissioning, including the personnel 
intended to accomplish each of the activities. 
2. A listing of the specific ESS and associated 
components, controls and safety-related 
devices to be tested, a description of the tests 
to be performed and the functions to be 
tested. 
3. Conditions under which all testing will be 
performed, which are representative of the 
conditions during normal operation of the 
system. 
4. Documentation of the owner′s project 
requirements and the basis of design 
necessary to understand the installation and 
operation of the ESS. 
-

section 
covers

requirements, 
which 
are

considerations 
for 
providing 
a 
safe,

and

of newly installed ESS and existing ESS that have 
been 
retrofitted, 
replaced 
or

-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1019 
 
5. Verification that required equipment and 
systems are installed in accordance with the 
approved plans and specifications. 
6. Integrated testing for all fire and safety 
systems. 
7. Testing 
for 
any 
required 
thermal 
management, ventilation or exhaust systems 
associated with the ESS installation. 
8. Preparation and delivery of operation and 
maintenance documentation. 
9. Training 
of 
facility 
operating 
and 
maintenance staff. 
10. Identification and documentation of the 
requirements 
for 
maintaining 
system 
performance to meet the original design 
intent during the operation phase. 
11. Identification 
and 
documentation 
of 
personnel who are qualified to service, 
maintain and decommission the ESS, and 
respond to incidents involving the ESS, 
including documentation that such service 
has been contracted for. 
12. A decommissioning plan for removing the 
ESS from service, and from the facility in 
which it is located. The plan shall include 
details on providing a safe, orderly shutdown 
of energy storage and safety systems with 
notification to the building and fire officials 
prior to the actual decommissioning of the 
system. The decommissioning plan shall 
include contingencies for removing an intact 
operational ESS from service, and for 
removing an ESS from service that has been 
damaged by a fire or other event. 
Exception: Commissioning shall not be required 
for leadacid and nickel-cadmium battery systems at 
facilities 
under 
the 
exclusive 
control 
of 
communications utilities that comply with NFPA 
76 and operate at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC. A 
decommissioning plan shall be provided and 
maintained where required by the Fire official. 
❖ 
The main intent of this section is to make sure 
that an ESS installation will perform as 
designed safely. The requirements of this 
section are best practice requirements that 
communicate how the system is to work and 
verify that it is in fact operating as designed. 
This process inherently reduces the risk of 
system failure. This includes the need to 
provide a plan for decommissioning from the 
very start to properly address the lifecycle of 
the system. 
1207.2.1.1 
Initial acceptance testing. During 
the commissioning process an ESS shall be 
evaluated for proper operation in accordance with 
the 
manufacturer′s 
instructions 
and 
the 
commissioning plan prior to final approval. 
❖ 
This section ensures that the verification 
process occurs and that such process is 
consistent 
with 
both 
the 
manufacturer′s 
instructions and established commissioning 
plan. 
1207.2.1.2 
Commissioning report. A report 
describing the results of the system commissioning, 
including the results of the initial acceptance testing 
required in Section 1207.2.1.1, shall be provided to 
the Fire official prior to final inspection and 
approval and maintained at an approved on-site 
location. 
❖ 
❖ This section requires that the commissioning 
process is documented prior to final approval. 
This report may be critical in the future and 
needs to be placed in an approved on-site 
location. The location of the commissioning 
report will vary and will need to be specific to 
each installation. It should be removed from 
any potentially hazardous location but needs to 
be convenient to the ESS installation. There is 
also the possibility it could be electronically 
available; however, this section does not go 
into that level of specificity. 
1207.2.2 
Operation and maintenance. An 
operation and maintenance manual shall be 
provided to both the ESS owner or their authorized 
agent and the ESS operator before the ESS is put 
into operation and shall include the following: 
1. Manufacturer′s 
operation 
manuals 
and 
maintenance manuals for the entire ESS, or 
for each component of the system requiring 
maintenance, that clearly identify the 
required routine maintenance actions. 
2. Name, address and phone number of a 
service agency that has been contracted to 
service the ESS and its associated safety 
systems. 
3. Maintenance and calibration information, 
including wiring diagrams, control drawings, 
schematics, 
system 
programming 
instructions 
and 
control 
sequence 
descriptions, for all energy storage control 
systems. 
4. Desired or field-determined control set 
points that are permanently recorded on 
-

-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1020 
 
control drawings at control devices or, for 
digital 
control 
systems, 
in 
system 
programming instructions. 
5. A schedule for inspecting and recalibrating 
all ESS controls. 
6. A service record log form that lists the 
schedule for all required servicing and 
maintenance actions and space for logging 
such actions that are completed over time and 
retained on-site. 
7. The ESS shall be operated and maintained in 
accordance with the manual and a copy of the 
manual shall be retained at an approved on-
site location. 
❖ 
This manual is part of the package of 
requirements that ensures proper operation 
and maintenance to reduce the likelihood of a 
failure. 
All 
the 
pertinent 
information 
is 
contained in this manual for ongoing operation, 
including the contact information for those who 
do maintenance or repairs on the system. The 
schedule for inspecting and recalibrating ESS 
controls is included along with a service log to 
ensure the system is properly maintained. 
Similar to the commissioning report, a copy of 
the manual needs to be located in an approved 
on-site location. See the commentary to 
Section 1207.2.1.2. 
1207.2.2.1 
Ongoing inspection and testing. 
Systems that monitor and protect the ESS 
installation shall be inspected and tested in 
accordance with the manufacturer′s instructions and 
the operation and maintenance manual. Inspection 
and testing records shall be maintained in the 
operation and maintenance manual. 
❖ 
In addition to the ESS itself, systems such as 
gas detection, ventilation, smoke detection and 
automatic 
sprinklers 
also 
need 
to 
be 
maintained to assist in mitigating any failures. 
These records also need to be included in the 
operation and maintenance manual. 
1207.2.3 
Decommissioning. 
The 
fire 
official 
shall 
be 
notified 
prior 
to 
the 
decommissioning of an ESS. Decommissioning 
shall be performed in accordance with the 
decommissioning plan that includes the following: 
1. A narrative description of the activities to be 
accomplished for removing the ESS from 
service, and from the facility in which it is 
located. 
2. A listing of any contingencies for removing 
an intact operational ESS from service, and 
for removing an ESS from service that has 
been damaged by a fire or other event. 
❖ 
Decommissioning 
is 
as 
critical 
as 
commissioning when it comes to ESS. Such 
systems are subject to thermal runaway even 
when they are no longer in use. Therefore, they 
must be properly removed from service, which 
typically means removal from the facility. The 
requirements will vary based on if it is a 
functioning system or one that is damaged. 
Though the commissioning report is required to 
contain a decommissioning report for the ESS, 
in accordance with Section 1207.2.1, it is 
recognized that if decommissioning is due to 
damage from fire or other event, the specific 
details of safe decommissioning will be incident 
specific. 
1207.3 Equipment. ESS equipment shall be in 
accordance 
with 
Sections 
1207.3.1 
through 
1207.3.9. 
❖ 
This section covers the ESS equipment 
requirements, including listings. This section 
includes requirements related to repairs, 
retrofits and replacements when systems need 
to be upgraded or serviced. Such guidance is 
necessary with rapidly changing technologies 
and the need to make sure that ESS operate 
as optimally as possible. 
1207.3.1 
Energy storage system listings. 
ESS shall be listed in accordance with UL 9540. 
Exception: Lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery 
systems installed in facilities under the exclusive 
control of communications utilities, and operating 
at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC in accordance 
with NFPA 76, are not required to be listed. 
❖ 
Energy storage systems are required to be 
listed in accordance with UL 9540. This 
standard covers the listing of storage batteries 
and all the equipment associated with the 
system, including cabinets and prefabricated 
enclosures. 
 
The 
exception 
recognizes 
the 
proven 
technology of lead-acid and nickel-cadmium 
battery 
systems 
as 
operated 
through 
communications utilities that comply with 
NFPA 76. 
1207.3.2 
Equipment 
listing. 
Chargers, 
inverters and energy storage management systems 
shall be covered as part of the UL 9540 listing or 
shall be listed separately. 
❖ 
There are separate listings that address 
chargers, inverters and energy management 
systems where they are not part of the UL 9540 
listing. If they are not part of the UL 9540 listing, 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1020 
 
control drawings at control devices or, for 
digital 
control 
systems, 
in 
system 
programming instructions. 
5. A schedule for inspecting and recalibrating 
all ESS controls. 
6. A service record log form that lists the 
schedule for all required servicing and 
maintenance actions and space for logging 
such actions that are completed over time and 
retained on-site. 
7. The ESS shall be operated and maintained in 
accordance with the manual and a copy of the 
manual shall be retained at an approved on-
site location.
pages.commentary:
This manual is part of the package of 
requirements that ensures proper operation 
and maintenance to reduce the likelihood of a 
failure. 
All 
the 
pertinent 
information 
is 
contained in this manual for ongoing operation, 
including the contact information for those who 
do maintenance or repairs on the system. The 
schedule for inspecting and recalibrating ESS 
controls is included along with a service log to 
ensure the system is properly maintained. 
Similar to the commissioning report, a copy of 
the manual needs to be located in an approved 
on-site location. See the commentary to 
Section 1207.2.1.2. 
1207.2.2.1 
Ongoing inspection and testing. 
Systems that monitor and protect the ESS 
installation shall be inspected and tested in 
accordance with the manufacturer′s instructions and 
the operation and maintenance manual. Inspection 
and testing records shall be maintained in the 
operation and maintenance manual.

❖ In addition to the ESS itself, systems such as 
gas detection, ventilation, smoke detection and 
automatic 
sprinklers 
also 
need 
to 
be 
maintained to assist in mitigating any failures. 
These records also need to be included in the 
operation and maintenance manual.

The 
fire 
official 
shall 
be 
notified 
prior 
to 
the 
decommissioning of an ESS. Decommissioning 
shall be performed in accordance with the 
decommissioning plan that includes the following: 
1. A narrative description of the activities to be 
accomplished for removing the ESS from 
service, and from the facility in which it is 
located. 
2. A listing of any contingencies for removing 
an intact operational ESS from service, and 
for removing an ESS from service that has 
been damaged by a fire or other event.

❖ Decommissioning 
is 
as 
critical 
as 
commissioning when it comes to ESS. Such 
systems are subject to thermal runaway even 
when they are no longer in use. Therefore, they 
must be properly removed from service, which 
typically means removal from the facility. The 
requirements will vary based on if it is a 
functioning system or one that is damaged. 
Though the commissioning report is required to 
contain a decommissioning report for the ESS, 
in accordance with Section 1207.2.1, it is 
recognized that if decommissioning is due to 
damage from fire or other event, the specific 
details of safe decommissioning will be incident 
specific.

accordance 
with 
Sections 
1207.3.1 
through 
1207.3.9.

❖ This section covers the ESS equipment 
requirements, including listings. This section 
includes requirements related to repairs, 
retrofits and replacements when systems need 
to be upgraded or serviced. Such guidance is 
necessary with rapidly changing technologies 
and the need to make sure that ESS operate 
as optimally as possible. 
1207.3.1 
Energy storage system listings. 
ESS shall be listed in accordance with UL 9540. 
Exception: Lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery 
systems installed in facilities under the exclusive 
control of communications utilities, and operating 
at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC in accordance 
with NFPA 76, are not required to be listed.

listed in accordance with UL 9540. This 
standard covers the listing of storage batteries 
and all the equipment associated with the 
system, including cabinets and prefabricated 
enclosures. 
 
The 
exception 
recognizes 
the 
proven 
technology of lead-acid and nickel-cadmium 
battery 
systems 
as 
operated 
through 
communications utilities that comply with 
NFPA 76. 
1207.3.2 
Equipment 
listing. 
Chargers, 
inverters and energy storage management systems 
shall be covered as part of the UL 9540 listing or 
shall be listed separately.

❖ There are separate listings that address 
chargers, inverters and energy management 
systems where they are not part of the UL 9540 
listing. If they are not part of the UL 9540 listing, 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1021 
 
they must be addressed individually by specific 
listing standards. 
1207.3.3 
Utility 
interactive 
systems. 
Inverters shall be listed and labeled in accordance 
with UL 1741. Only inverters listed and labeled for 
utility interactive system use and identified as 
interactive shall be allowed to operate in parallel 
with the electric utility power system to supply 
power to common loads. 
❖ 
This section requires a listing in accordance 
with UL 1741 where it is not part of the UL 9540 
listing. An inverter is an electronic device that 
is capable of changing direct current (DC) to 
alternating current (AC). Inverters need to be 
specifically listed and labeled to avoid using the 
wrong type and subsequently leading to 
system failure. Note that this section also 
requires a specific listing for utility interactive 
systems. These types of inverters allow things 
like automatic transfer to enable the ESS to 
feed power back into the commercial power 
grid or disconnect from the commercial grid 
when it is supposed to be de-energized due to 
an outage. These types of inverters must be 
inspected by the electrical inspector and the 
power utility in most cases. 
1207.3.4 
Energy 
storage 
management 
system. Where required by the ESS listing, an 
approved energy storage management system that 
monitors and balances cell voltages, currents and 
temperatures 
within 
the 
manufacturer′s 
specifications shall be provided. The system shall 
disconnect electrical connections to the ESS or 
otherwise place it in a safe condition if potentially 
hazardous temperatures or other conditions such as 
short circuits, over voltage or under voltage are 
detected. 
❖ 
An energy management system is an important 
safety system that monitors and takes actions 
on abnormal conditions that could lead to 
failures. These systems are critical as part of 
the risk reduction. 
1207.3.5 
Enclosures. Enclosures of ESS 
shall be of noncombustible construction. 
❖ 
This prevents other methods of fire spread from 
one ESS to another or other combustibles 
within a building. 
1207.3.6 
Repairs. Repairs of ESS shall only 
be done by qualified personnel. Repairs with other 
than identical parts shall be considered retrofitting 
and comply with Section 1207.3.7. Repairs shall be 
documented in the service records log. 
❖ 
Several requirements are addressed in this 
section. The first is the necessary qualifications 
of those undertaking the repairs. This is 
especially important given that these systems 
are complex and at risk of failure if the repairs 
are 
done 
incorrectly. 
Second, 
system 
innovations 
are 
happening 
quickly, 
and 
identical parts may not be available. To ensure 
compatibility in those cases, the replacement 
parts and equipment need to be addressed as 
a retrofit in accordance with Section 1207.3.7. 
This section requires all repairs to be 
documented. 
1207.3.7 
Retrofits. 
Retrofitting 
of 
an 
existing ESS shall comply with the following: 
1. A construction permit shall be obtained in 
accordance with Section 105.6.5. 
2. New batteries, battery modules, capacitors 
and similar ESS components shall be listed. 
3. Battery management and other monitoring 
systems shall be connected and installed in 
accordance 
with 
the 
manufacturer′s 
instructions. 
4. The overall installation shall continue to 
comply with UL 9540 listing requirements, 
where applicable. 
5. Systems that have been retrofitted shall be 
commissioned in accordance with Section 
1207.2.1. 
6. Retrofits shall be documented in the service 
records log. 
❖ 
This section addresses practices to be followed 
when systems need to be upgraded or 
serviced. These systems are very sensitive to 
changes that could lead to thermal runaway, so 
clear requirements for retrofit need to be 
outlined. This includes obtaining a construction 
permit to notify the fire department  a retrofit is 
occurring. In addition, batteries must be 
properly listed and the overall installation must 
comply with UL 9540. Finally, the system 
needs to be commissioned to demonstrate 
performance, and records of the process need 
to be provided. 
1207.3.7.1 
Retrofitting lead acid and nickel 
cadmium. Section 1207.3.7 shall not apply to 
retrofitting of lead-acid and nickel-cadmium 
batteries with other lead-acid and nickelcadmium 
batteries at facilities under the exclusive control of 
communications utilities that comply with NFPA 
76 and operate at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC. 
❖ 
❖ These technologies, where small in 
capacity, are sufficiently regulated and have 
performed 
safely 
under 
the 
control 
of 
-

1021 
 
they must be addressed individually by specific 
listing standards.

cadmium. Section 1207.3.7 shall not apply to

76 and operate at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC.

❖ 

❖ These technologies, where small in 
capacity, are sufficiently regulated and have 
performed 
safely 
under 
the 
control 
of 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1022 
 
communications utilities that comply with 
NFPA 76. This exception is technology 
specific; for example, if a lead-acid battery was 
replaced with a lithium-ion battery, that would 
not be considered a retrofit. The change in 
battery technology would require the system to 
be treated as new, and all appropriate 
requirements of Section 1207 would apply. 
1207.3.8 
Replacements. Replacements of 
ESS shall be considered new ESS installations and 
shall comply with the provisions of Section 1207 as 
applicable to new ESS. The ESS being replaced 
shall be decommissioned in accordance with 
Section 1207.2.3. 
❖ 
This section requires that replacement of ESS 
shall be addressed no differently than as new 
and Section 1207 would apply to this 
installation. Where an ESS system is being 
replaced, it is important that such systems are 
properly decommissioned since the existing 
system may still be energized or subject to 
thermal runaway. 
1207.3.9 
Reused 
and 
repurposed 
equipment. Equipment and materials shall only be 
reused or reinstalled as permitted in Section 
104.8.1. Storage batteries previously used in other 
applications, such as electric vehicle propulsion, 
shall not be reused in applications regulated by 
Chapter 12 unless approved by the Fire official and 
unless the equipment is refurbished by a battery 
refurbishing company approved in accordance with 
UL 1974. 
❖ 
This section allows Fire officials to regulate 
installations of repurposed electric vehicle 
batteries that are converted for ESS use in 
buildings. It is important to recognize that UL 
1974 applies to the process used by the firm 
refurbishing the battery. It is not a listing of the 
battery; once refurbished, the use of such 
batteries must be in compliance with Section 
1207, including battery and system listing 
requirements. 
1207.4 General 
installations 
requirements. 
Stationary and mobile ESS shall comply with the 
requirements 
of 
Sections 
1207.4.1 
through 
1207.4.12. 
❖ 
This section is focused on a number of 
requirements that facilitate the safe operation 
of ESS such as proper electrical disconnects, 
clearances, seismic supports and separation 
from combustibles. These features are part of 
the package of safety measures that reduce 
risk of a failure, which may lead to hazardous 
conditions. 
1207.4.1 
Electrical disconnects. Where the 
ESS disconnecting means is not within sight of the 
main electrical service disconnecting means, 
placards or directories shall be installed at the 
location of the main electrical service disconnecting 
means indicating the location of stationary storage 
battery system disconnecting means in accordance 
with SBC 401. 
Exception: Electrical disconnects for lead-acid and 
nickelcadmium battery systems at facilities under 
the exclusive control of communications utilities 
and operating at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC 
shall be permitted to have electrical disconnects 
signage in accordance with NFPA 76. 
❖ 
Stationary storage battery systems may be in 
various locations within buildings and may not 
be 
near 
the 
main 
service 
disconnect. 
Therefore, a way of communicating the 
disconnect for such systems is necessary at 
the main service disconnect. This is critical to 
fighting a fire and being able to disconnect all 
power 
supplies 
affecting 
fire-fighting 
operations. Note that to ensure reliability of the 
telecommunications network, batteries utilized 
in telecommunications power plants in central 
offices rarely have circuit breakers or an 
automatic disconnecting means in order to 
satisfy an FCC Best Practice designed to 
ensure the highest reliability. Such installations 
are not within the scope of SBC 401, and a 
disconnecting means is not required. 
1207.4.2 
Working clearances. Access and 
working space shall be provided and maintained 
about all electrical equipment to permit ready and 
safe operation and maintenance of such equipment 
in accordance with SBC 401 and the manufacturer′s 
instructions. 
❖ 
This section requires the proper spacing to 
reduce ignition of surrounding materials and to 
allow proper maintenance. This is consistent 
with Section 603.4. 
1207.4.3 
Fire-resistance-rated 
separations. Rooms and other indoor areas 
containing ESS shall be separated from other areas 
of the building in accordance with Section 1207.7.4. 
ESS shall be permitted to be in the same room with 
the equipment they support. 
❖ 
This section focuses on containment of the 
hazard to the location where the ESS is 
located, whether in an entire room or in a small 
space such as a closet. ESS being subject to 
thermal runaway could cause a fire that will 
continue to burn for an extended amount of 
time. Section 1207.7.4 requires 2-hour fire 
-

CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1022 
 
communications utilities that comply with 
NFPA 76. This exception is technology 
specific; for example, if a lead-acid battery was 
replaced with a lithium-ion battery, that would 
not be considered a retrofit. The change in 
battery technology would require the system to 
be treated as new, and all appropriate 
requirements of Section 1207 would apply.

ESS shall be considered new ESS installations and 
shall comply with the provisions of Section 1207 as 
applicable to new ESS. The ESS being replaced 
shall be decommissioned in accordance with 
Section 1207.2.3.

requirements 
of 
Sections 
1207.4.1 
through 
1207.4.12.

conditions.

power 
supplies

separations. Rooms and other indoor areas 
containing ESS shall be separated from other areas 
of the building in accordance with Section 1207.7.4.

the equipment they support.

❖ This section focuses on containment of the 
hazard to the location where the ESS is 
located, whether in an entire room or in a small 
space such as a closet. ESS being subject to 
thermal runaway could cause a fire that will 
continue to burn for an extended amount of 
time. Section 1207.7.4 requires 2-hour fire 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1023 
 
barriers and 2-hour horizontal separations. In 
many cases, due to the impracticality of 
separating ESS from the equipment being 
supported, it is clarified that they may remain 
together in the same room. 
1207.4.4 
Seismic and structural design. 
Stationary ESS shall comply with the seismic 
design requirements in Chapter 16 of SBC 201, and 
shall not exceed the floor loading limitation of the 
building. 
❖ 
Some of these installations are quite heavy, so 
much so that they can potentially affect the 
structural integrity of the building and the floor 
loading limitations need to be addressed. Also, 
to avoid additional failures during seismic 
events, the requirements of SBC 201 must be 
addressed. Assessment of rooftop installations 
on existing buildings must include whether 
structural fire resistance of the primary 
structural frame and bearing walls will remain 
in compliance with SBC 201 Table 601. 
1207.4.5 
Vehicle 
impact 
protection. 
Where ESS are subject to impact by a motor 
vehicle, including fork lifts, vehicle impact 
protection shall be provided in accordance with 
Section 312. 
❖ 
This section provides the requirements for 
protecting the battery system from physical 
damage to avoid a malfunction of the systems 
and the hazards that could result. This applies 
to indoor or outdoor installations, depending on 
the potential impact risk from motor vehicles 
(see commentary, Section 312). 
1207.4.6 
Combustible 
storage. 
Combustible materials shall not be stored in ESS 
rooms, areas or walk-in units. Combustible 
materials in occupied work centers covered by 
Section 1207.4.10 shall be stored at least 900 mm 
from ESS cabinets. 
❖ 
In the past, ESS were often independent of the 
rest of the building and its operations. In 
particular, the focus had only been on 
applications 
such 
as 
telecommunications 
buildings. However, there are many cases now 
where ESS are located in a variety of locations 
with other ongoing activities, such as data 
centers. This section requires a minimum 
separation from combustibles or prohibits the 
storage of combustible materials to reduce the 
risk of fire. 
1207.4.7 
Toxic and highly toxic gases. ESS 
that have the potential to release toxic and highly 
toxic gas during charging, discharging and normal 
use conditions shall be provided with a hazardous 
exhaust system in accordance with Section 502.8 of 
SBC 501. 
❖ 
This section covers battery technologies (many 
yet to hit the market) that may potentially 
produce 
toxic 
gases 
during 
charging, 
discharging and normal use, and trigger 
Chapter 60 safety requirements. There is no 
intent to address batteries that produce toxic 
gases during fires or abnormal conditions. 
Hydrogen and oxygen produced by vented 
batteries are not considered toxic or highly 
toxic gases. 
1207.4.8 
Signage. Approved signs shall be 
provided on or adjacent to all entry doors for ESS 
rooms or areas and on enclosures of ESS cabinets 
and walk-in units located outdoors, on rooftops or 
in open parking garages. Signs designed to meet 
both the requirements of this section and SBC 401 
shall be permitted. The signage shall include the 
following or equivalent: 
1.  “ENERGY 
STORAGE 
SYSTEM,” 
“BATTERY 
STORAGE 
SYSTEM,” 
“CAPACITOR 
ENERGY 
STORAGE 
SYSTEM” or the equivalent. 
2. The identification of the electrochemical ESS 
technology present. 
3.  “ENERGIZED 
ELECTRICAL 
CIRCUITS.” 
4. Where water-reactive electrochemical ESS 
are present, the signage shall include 
“APPLY NO WATER.” 
5. Current contact information, including phone 
number, for personnel authorized to service 
the equipment and for fire mitigation 
personnel required by Section 1207.1.6.1. 
Exception: Existing electrochemical ESS shall be 
permitted to include the signage required at the time 
they were installed. 
❖ 
ESS are found in varying applications in 
today′s world. They are no longer simply for 
standby 
and 
emergency 
power, 
and 
technologies are varied and evolving quickly. 
 
This section identifies the need for signage to 
identify the hazards. Items 1 and 2 require 
signage to denote where ESS are contained in 
a room and the type of technology present. 
Item 3 focuses on communicating through 
signage that energized electrical circuits are 
present. 
 
Item 4 provides necessary information to 
occupants and emergency responders of any 
water-reactive 
properties 
and 
clearly 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1023 
 
barriers and 2-hour horizontal separations. In 
many cases, due to the impracticality of 
separating ESS from the equipment being 
supported, it is clarified that they may remain 
together in the same room. 
1207.4.4 
Seismic and structural design. 
Stationary ESS shall comply with the seismic 
design requirements in Chapter 16 of SBC 201, and 
shall not exceed the floor loading limitation of the 
building.
pages.commentary:
Some of these installations are quite heavy, so 
much so that they can potentially affect the 
structural integrity of the building and the floor 
loading limitations need to be addressed. Also, 
to avoid additional failures during seismic 
events, the requirements of SBC 201 must be 
addressed. Assessment of rooftop installations 
on existing buildings must include whether 
structural fire resistance of the primary 
structural frame and bearing walls will remain 
in compliance with SBC 201 Table 601. 
1207.4.5 
Vehicle 
impact 
protection. 
Where ESS are subject to impact by a motor 
vehicle, including fork lifts, vehicle impact 
protection shall be provided in accordance with 
Section 312.

❖ This section provides the requirements for 
protecting the battery system from physical 
damage to avoid a malfunction of the systems 
and the hazards that could result. This applies 
to indoor or outdoor installations, depending on 
the potential impact risk from motor vehicles 
(see commentary, Section 312). 
1207.4.6 
Combustible 
storage. 
Combustible materials shall not be stored in ESS 
rooms, areas or walk-in units. Combustible 
materials in occupied work centers covered by 
Section 1207.4.10 shall be stored at least 900 mm 
from ESS cabinets.

❖ In the past, ESS were often independent of the 
rest of the building and its operations. In 
particular, the focus had only been on 
applications 
such 
as 
telecommunications 
buildings. However, there are many cases now 
where ESS are located in a variety of locations 
with other ongoing activities, such as data 
centers. This section requires a minimum 
separation from combustibles or prohibits the 
storage of combustible materials to reduce the 
risk of fire. 
1207.4.7 
Toxic and highly toxic gases. ESS 
that have the potential to release toxic and highly 
toxic gas during charging, discharging and normal 
use conditions shall be provided with a hazardous 
exhaust system in accordance with Section 502.8 of 
SBC 501.

❖ This section covers battery technologies (many 
yet to hit the market) that may potentially 
produce 
toxic 
gases 
during 
charging, 
discharging and normal use, and trigger 
Chapter 60 safety requirements. There is no 
intent to address batteries that produce toxic 
gases during fires or abnormal conditions. 
Hydrogen and oxygen produced by vented 
batteries are not considered toxic or highly 
toxic gases. 
1207.4.8 
Signage. Approved signs shall be 
provided on or adjacent to all entry doors for ESS 
rooms or areas and on enclosures of ESS cabinets 
and walk-in units located outdoors, on rooftops or 
in open parking garages. Signs designed to meet 
both the requirements of this section and SBC 401 
shall be permitted. The signage shall include the 
following or equivalent: 
1.  “ENERGY 
STORAGE 
SYSTEM,” 
“BATTERY 
STORAGE 
SYSTEM,” 
“CAPACITOR 
ENERGY 
STORAGE 
SYSTEM” or the equivalent. 
2. The identification of the electrochemical ESS 
technology present. 
3.  “ENERGIZED 
ELECTRICAL 
CIRCUITS.” 
4. Where water-reactive electrochemical ESS 
are present, the signage shall include 
“APPLY NO WATER.” 
5. Current contact information, including phone 
number, for personnel authorized to service 
the equipment and for fire mitigation 
personnel required by Section 1207.1.6.1. 
Exception: Existing electrochemical ESS shall be 
permitted to include the signage required at the time 
they were installed.

❖ ESS are found in varying applications in 
today′s world. They are no longer simply for 
standby 
and 
emergency 
power, 
and 
technologies are varied and evolving quickly. 
 
This section identifies the need for signage to 
identify the hazards. Items 1 and 2 require 
signage to denote where ESS are contained in 
a room and the type of technology present. 
Item 3 focuses on communicating through 
signage that energized electrical circuits are 
present. 
 
Item 4 provides necessary information to 
occupants and emergency responders of any 
water-reactive 
properties 
and 
clearly 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1024 
 
communicates through signage that water 
cannot be applied. 
 
Item 5 addresses the need to provide contact 
information regarding personnel authorized to 
service the ESS and more importantly, fire 
mitigation personnel who are properly trained 
to manage the hazards associated with the 
ESS. See Section 1207.1.6.1. 
 
The exception addresses currently installed 
systems that were not required to have the 
prescribed signage at the time of installation, 
which often will be standalone lead-acid battery 
systems. These types of installations have had 
a good safety record and typically do not create 
exposure hazards based on their chemistries 
and the way they are used and regulated. 
1207.4.9 
Security of installations. Rooms, 
areas and walk-in units in which electrochemical 
ESS are located shall be secured against 
unauthorized entry and safeguarded in an approved 
manner. Security barriers, fences, landscaping and 
other enclosures shall not inhibit the required air 
flow to or exhaust from the electrochemical ESS 
and its components. 
❖ 
This section is general to all ESS installation 
types, whether indoors or outdoors. Properly 
securing such installations reduces the risk of 
arson or vandalism, which could lead to a 
failure of such systems. This is more of a 
concern with outdoor installations or walk-in 
units. It is important that the security features 
do not negatively impact the ventilation 
requirements for the installation. Facilities with 
a secured perimeter and controlled access to 
the site would qualify, and additional securing 
of an ESS installation on such a site would not 
be required. 
1207.4.10 
Occupied 
work 
centers. 
Electrochemical ESS located in rooms or areas 
occupied by personnel not directly involved with 
maintenance, service and testing of the systems 
shall comply with the following: 
1. Electrochemical ESS located in occupied 
work centers shall be housed in locked 
noncombustible cabinets or other enclosures 
to prevent access by unauthorized personnel. 
2. Where electrochemical ESS are contained in 
cabinets in occupied work centers, the 
cabinets shall be located within 3 m of the 
equipment that they support. 
3. Cabinets shall include signage complying 
with Section 1207.4.8. 
❖ 
In cases where the ESS is located where 
building occupants could be present, some 
level of protection is necessary. 
 
This section first requires the ESS to be 
secured. Similar to Section 1207.4.9, this 
reduces a possible failure of the system due to 
vandalism or arson. 
 
Placing battery system cabinets within 3 m of 
the equipment served by the system will limit 
the number of devices that can be powered by 
the system due to the voltage drop associated 
with longer or multiple conductor runs. This 
section also enhances the safety of personnel 
who may have to secure and work on 
equipment served by the battery system by 
placing the secondary power supply near 
enough to the powered equipment to ensure 
line-of-sight supervision of the secured battery 
system 
during 
equipment 
repair 
or 
maintenance. 
 
This section calls for the identification of the 
specific hazards associated with the ESS 
contained in each cabinet. ESS technologies 
vary and with that, the hazard varies. This 
information will also provide the capacity of 
each system, which is essential for the safety 
of the occupants and first responders. 
1207.4.11 
Open rack installations. Where 
electrochemical ESS are installed in a separate 
equipment room and only authorized personnel 
have access to the room, they shall be permitted to 
be installed on an open rack for ease of 
maintenance. 
❖ 
This section essentially allows ESS to be on 
open racks where access to the room is 
restricted. 
The 
purpose 
is 
to 
facilitate 
maintenance, which could have the effect of 
better safety performance. Essentially, the 
ESS utilizes the room as a means of separation 
in place of cabinet enclosures. 
1207.4.12 
Walk-in units. Walk-in units shall 
be entered only for inspection, maintenance and 
repair of ESS units and ancillary equipment, and 
shall not be occupied for other purposes. 
❖ 
This section clarifies the intent of walk-in units. 
Such units are not intended for regular 
occupancy and are only to be entered for the 
purposes of maintenance and repairs. Those 
entering are trained to do so. Although 
occupancy is restricted, ESS walk-in units are 
defined as buildings, and all requirements 
applicable to buildings apply, including fire 
suppression protection. 
1207.5 Electrochemical ESS protection. The 
protection of electrochemical ESS shall be in 
accordance 
with 
Sections 
1207.5.1 
through 
-

be entered only for inspection, maintenance and 
repair of ESS units and ancillary equipment, and 
shall not be occupied for other purposes.

Such units are not intended for regular 
occupancy and are only to be entered for the 
purposes of maintenance and repairs. Those 
entering are trained to do so. Although 
occupancy is restricted, ESS walk-in units are 
defined as buildings, and all requirements 
applicable to buildings apply, including fire 
suppression protection.

protection of electrochemical ESS shall be in 
accordance 
with 
Sections 
1207.5.1 
through

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1025 
 
1207.5.8 where required by Sections 1207.7 
through 1207.10. 
❖ 
This section generally sets out the specific 
protection required for the ESS itself. Section 
1207.4 focuses on the elements surrounding 
such ESS but not the specific protection of the 
equipment. The triggers for these protection 
features vary based on the technology used 
and the type of installation addressed in 
Sections 1207.7 through 1207.10 (indoor 
installations, outdoor installations and special 
installations such as rooftop installations and 
mobile ESS equipment). 
 
This table sets out the MAQ of each technology 
that can be found in a single installation within 
rooms, areas and walk-in units. This serves as 
a trigger to the application of Section 1207.1.5 
and large-scale fire tests, which will provide 
important data necessary for the determination 
of effective protection features (see Section 
1207.5.2, Item 1). Certain technologies are 
unlimited in capacity based on their historically 
good safety record. This does not exempt such 
technologies from protection; it is simply the 
size of the installation that is not limited. 
1207.5.1 
Size 
and 
separation. 
Electrochemical ESS shall be segregated into 
groups not exceeding 50 kWh (180 megajoules). 
Each group shall be separated a minimum of 914 
mm from other groups and from walls in the storage 
room or area. The storage arrangements shall 
comply with Chapter 10. 
Exceptions: 
1. Lead-acid 
and 
nickel-cadmium 
battery 
systems in facilities under the exclusive 
control of communications utilities and 
operating at less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC 
in accordance with NFPA 76. 
2. The Fire official is authorized to approve 
larger capacities or smaller separation 
distances based on large-scale fire testing 
complying with Section 1207.1.5. 
❖ 
In an effort to reduce the possible size and 
intensity of a fire or explosion, ESS units are 
limited in capacity and must be separated into 
certain capacity groups. Each group must have 
a fire break of essentially 900 mm from other 
groups and walls. Exception1 acknowledges 
the safety record of lead-acid and nickel-
cadmium batteries and does not require the 50 
kWh and 900 mm separation. Exception 2 
provides flexibility to the base capacity of the 
ESS group if testing determines that a fire 
would be contained to the room as separated. 
It should also be noted that the types of 
installation that are addressed in Sections 
1207.7 through 1207.10 will affect whether 
these size and separation requirements apply. 
For example, Table 1207.8 for remote outdoor 
installations would not require compliance with 
this section. 
1207.5.2 
Maximum allowable quantities. 
Fire areas within rooms, areas and walk-in units 
containing electrochemical ESS shall not exceed 
the maximum allowable quantities in Table 1207.5. 
Exceptions: 
1. Where approved by the Fire official, rooms, 
areas 
and 
walk-in 
units 
containing 
electrochemical ESS that exceed the amounts 
in Table 1207.5 shall be permitted based on 
a 
hazardous 
mitigation 
analysis 
in 
accordance with Section 1207.1.4 and 
largescale fire testing complying with 
Section 1207.1.5. 
2. Lead-acid 
and 
nickel-cadmium 
battery 
systems installed in facilities under the 
exclusive 
control 
of 
communications 
utilities, and operating at less than 50 VAC 
and 60 VDC in accordance with NFPA 76. 
3. Dedicated-use buildings in compliance with 
Section 1207.7.1. 
❖ 
Scientific research and large-scale fire and 
fault condition testing are not available to justify 
allowing unlimited quantities of energy in an 
ESS to be provided in a single fire area. By 
referencing 
Table 
1207.5, 
this 
section 
establishes a maximum 600 kWh MAQ for 
each fire area and 200 kWh for certain 
technologies for which less information is 
available. The MAQ of 600 kWh is equivalent 
to 1,250 storage batteries, each rated 12 V, 40 
Ah. 
In 
addition, 
capacitors 
and 
other 
electrochemical ESS are limited to 20 kWh. 
 
There are several exceptions to these 
requirements. 
Exception 
1 
allows 
such 
installations to be reviewed through a hazards 
mitigation analysis and large-scale fire testing 
results to justify larger amounts of ESS. 
 
Exception 2 recognizes the safety record of 
leadacid and nickel-cadmium battery systems 
under the control of communications utilities. 
 
Exception 3 recognizes the minimal life safety 
hazard 
associated 
with 
dedicated-use 
buildings that comply with Section 1207.7.1. 
1207.5.2.1 
Mixed electrochemical energy 
systems. Where rooms, areas and walk-in units 
contain different types of electrochemical energy 
technologies, the total aggregate quantities of the 
-

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1025 
 
1207.5.8 where required by Sections 1207.7 
through 1207.10.

and 
walk-in 
units 
containing 
electrochemical ESS that exceed the amounts 
in Table 1207.5 shall be permitted based on 
a 
hazardous 
mitigation 
analysis 
in 
accordance with Section 1207.1.4 and 
largescale fire testing complying with 
Section 1207.1.5. 
2. Lead-acid 
and 
nickel-cadmium 
battery 
systems installed in facilities under the 
exclusive 
control 
of 
communications 
utilities, and operating at less than 50 VAC 
and 60 VDC in accordance with NFPA 76. 
3. Dedicated-use buildings in compliance with 
Section 1207.7.1.

❖ Scientific research and large-scale fire and 
fault condition testing are not available to justify 
allowing unlimited quantities of energy in an 
ESS to be provided in a single fire area. By 
referencing 
Table 
1207.5, 
this 
section 
establishes a maximum 600 kWh MAQ for 
each fire area and 200 kWh for certain 
technologies for which less information is 
available. The MAQ of 600 kWh is equivalent 
to 1,250 storage batteries, each rated 12 V, 40 
Ah. 
In 
addition, 
capacitors 
and 
other 
electrochemical ESS are limited to 20 kWh. 
 
There are several exceptions to these 
requirements. 
Exception 
1 
allows 
such 
installations to be reviewed through a hazards 
mitigation analysis and large-scale fire testing 
results to justify larger amounts of ESS. 
 
Exception 2 recognizes the safety record of 
leadacid and nickel-cadmium battery systems 
under the control of communications utilities. 
 
Exception 3 recognizes the minimal life safety 
hazard 
associated 
with 
dedicated-use 
buildings that comply with Section 1207.7.1. 
1207.5.2.1 
Mixed electrochemical energy 
systems. Where rooms, areas and walk-in units 
contain different types of electrochemical energy 
technologies, the total aggregate quantities of the 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1026 
 
systems shall be determined based on the sum of 
percentages of each technology-type quantity 
divided by the maximum allowable quantity of each 
technology type. The sum of the percentages shall 
not exceed 100 percent of the maximum allowable 
quantity. 
❖ 
Because of the changing technologies of ESS, 
it is becoming more common to have multiple 
technologies in a single building. This section 
addresses how to determine the MAQ where 
multiple ESS are used. Note that due to the 
safety record of lead-acid and nickel-cadmium 
battery systems, the MAQ are unlimited. If, for 
example, there are flow batteries and sodium 
batteries in the same fire area, the allowable 
installed quantity permitted under this section 
of the code is determined on a percentage 
basis as follows: 
 
Given: 
 
450 kWh flow battery system 
 
75 kWh other battery system 
 
450 kWh flow battery system = 450 kWh/600 
kWh = 0.75 × 100 = 75% 
 
75 kWh other battery technology system = 75 
kWh/200 kWh = 0.375 × 100 = 37.5% 
 
75% (flow) + 37.5% (other) = 112.5% of MAQ; 
Therefore, hazard mitigation analysis would be 
required 
for 
other 
than 
dedicated-use 
buildings. 
1207.5.3 
Elevation. Electrochemical ESS 
shall not be located in the following areas:  
1. Where the floor is located more than 23 m 
above the lowest level of fire department  
vehicle access. 
2. Where the floor is located below the lowest 
level of exit discharge. 
Exceptions: 
1. Lead-acid 
and 
nickel-cadmium 
battery 
systems less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC 
installed in facilities under the exclusive 
control of communications utilities in 
accordance with NFPA 76. 
2. Where approved, installations shall be 
permitted in underground vaults complying 
with SBC 401, Article 450, Part III. 
3. Where approved by the Fire official, 
installations shall be permitted on higher and 
lower floors. 
❖ 
In terms of location, the main concern is access 
for fire fighting and potential intensity of fires 
from certain ESS such as lithium ion. ESS are 
becoming more common in high-rise buildings 
and without this section, such installations can 
make it much more difficult for the fire 
department  to fight a fire. This section does not 
prohibit installations in high-rise buildings but 
instead limits the location of such systems to 
the low-rise portion of the building. A key 
component for fire attack is the amount of 
available water, and 23 m was chosen based 
on a fire department ′s ability to use pre-piped 
elevating tower ladders or ladders to bring a 
larger water supply to upper floors. Likewise, 
underground buildings also are addressed. 
Fires involving ESS can generate tremendous 
amounts of smoke and heat, which fire fighters 
would have to climb down into for floors lower 
than the level of exit discharge. These 
restrictions were specifically added based on 
concerns voiced by the operations side of the 
fire service. 
 
Exception 1 is provided for lead-acid and 
nickel-cadmium storage battery systems, as 
they have a good safety record and further 
restrictions beyond that provided in previous 
editions of this code are not necessary. 
 
Exception 2 allows installations in underground 
vaults that comply with SBC 401 that 
essentially provides 3 hours of fire-resistance-
rated construction. 
 
Exception 3 gives some flexibility to unique 
situations where the risks to such installations 
are properly mitigated and such installations 
are specifically approved. The amount of water 
available for fire fighting to floors over 23 m 
above the lowest level of fire department  
vehicle access would be a consideration for 
installations on higher floors. The ability to 
control and exhaust heat and smoke from 
lower floors would be a consideration for 
allowing installation on lower floors. 
1207.5.4 
Fire detection. An approved 
automatic smoke detection system or radiant 
energy-sensing fire detection system complying 
with Section 907.2 shall be installed in rooms, 
indoor areas and walk-in units containing 
electrochemical ESS. An approved radiant energy-
sensing fire detection system shall be installed to 
protect 
open 
parking 
garage 
and 
rooftop 
installations. Alarm signals from detection systems 
shall be transmitted to a central station, proprietary 
or remote station service in accordance with NFPA 
72, or where approved to a constantly attended 
location. 
❖ 
Many ESS fires are likely to be slow growing 
and smoldering; therefore, a smoke detection 
-

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1026 
 
systems shall be determined based on the sum of 
percentages of each technology-type quantity 
divided by the maximum allowable quantity of each 
technology type. The sum of the percentages shall 
not exceed 100 percent of the maximum allowable 
quantity.

above the lowest level of fire department  
vehicle access. 
2. Where the floor is located below the lowest 
level of exit discharge. 
Exceptions: 
1. Lead-acid 
and 
nickel-cadmium 
battery 
systems less than 50 VAC and 60 VDC 
installed in facilities under the exclusive 
control of communications utilities in 
accordance with NFPA 76. 
2. Where approved, installations shall be 
permitted in underground vaults complying 
with SBC 401, Article 450, Part III. 
3. Where approved by the Fire official, 
installations shall be permitted on higher and 
lower floors.

❖ In terms of location, the main concern is access 
for fire fighting and potential intensity of fires 
from certain ESS such as lithium ion. ESS are 
becoming more common in high-rise buildings 
and without this section, such installations can 
make it much more difficult for the fire 
department  to fight a fire. This section does not 
prohibit installations in high-rise buildings but 
instead limits the location of such systems to 
the low-rise portion of the building. A key 
component for fire attack is the amount of 
available water, and 23 m was chosen based 
on a fire department ′s ability to use pre-piped 
elevating tower ladders or ladders to bring a 
larger water supply to upper floors. Likewise, 
underground buildings also are addressed. 
Fires involving ESS can generate tremendous 
amounts of smoke and heat, which fire fighters 
would have to climb down into for floors lower 
than the level of exit discharge. These 
restrictions were specifically added based on 
concerns voiced by the operations side of the 
fire service. 
 
Exception 1 is provided for lead-acid and 
nickel-cadmium storage battery systems, as 
they have a good safety record and further 
restrictions beyond that provided in previous 
editions of this code are not necessary. 
 
Exception 2 allows installations in underground 
vaults that comply with SBC 401 that 
essentially provides 3 hours of fire-resistance-
rated construction. 
 
Exception 3 gives some flexibility to unique 
situations where the risks to such installations 
are properly mitigated and such installations 
are specifically approved. The amount of water 
available for fire fighting to floors over 23 m 
above the lowest level of fire department  
vehicle access would be a consideration for 
installations on higher floors. The ability to 
control and exhaust heat and smoke from 
lower floors would be a consideration for 
allowing installation on lower floors. 
1207.5.4 
Fire detection. An approved 
automatic smoke detection system or radiant 
energy-sensing fire detection system complying 
with Section 907.2 shall be installed in rooms, 
indoor areas and walk-in units containing 
electrochemical ESS. An approved radiant energy-
sensing fire detection system shall be installed to 
protect 
open 
parking 
garage 
and 
rooftop 
installations. Alarm signals from detection systems 
shall be transmitted to a central station, proprietary 
or remote station service in accordance with NFPA 
72, or where approved to a constantly attended 
location.

❖ Many ESS fires are likely to be slow growing 
and smoldering; therefore, a smoke detection 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1027 
 
system or radiant energy-sensing fire detection 
system is required for early detection and 
warning in accordance with Section 907.2 
where the system requires application of 
Section 1207 (i.e., exceeds the threshold 
quantities in Table 1207.1.1). All types of 
installations addressed by Sections 1207.7 
through 1207.10 require fire detection. For 
example, where an ESS using lithium-ion 
batteries exceeds 20 kWh, a smoke detection 
or radiant energy-sensing system must be 
provided. The goal is early warning of an event 
that the fire service needs to respond to. For 
radiant-energy detection, that would equate to 
thermal imaging or multi-spectrum infrared 
flame detectors, both of which are recognized 
by NFPA 72. 
1207.5.4.1 
System status. Where required by 
the Fire official, visible annunciation shall be 
provided on cabinet exteriors or in other approved 
locations to indicate that potentially hazardous 
conditions associated with the ESS exist. 
❖ 
This section provides authority to the Fire 
official 
to require 
visible 
notification 
in 
situations where it is deemed necessary to alert 
emergency 
responders 
directly 
of 
the 
conditions at the location of the hazard. A 
constantly attended location may not be at the 
equipment itself. 
1207.5.5 
Fire suppression systems. Rooms 
and areas within buildings and walk-in units 
containing electrochemical ESS shall be protected 
by an automatic fire suppression system designed 
and installed in accordance with one of the 
following: 
1. An automatic sprinkler system designed and 
installed 
in 
accordance 
with 
Section 
903.3.1.1 with a minimum density of 1.15 
L/min based on the fire area or 230 m2 design 
area, whichever is smaller. 
2. Where approved, an automatic sprinkler 
system designed and installed in accordance 
with Section 903.3.1.1 with a sprinkler 
hazard classification based on large-scale fire 
testing complying with Section 1207.1.5. 
3. The following alternative automatic fire-
extinguishing systems designed and installed 
in accordance with Section 904, provided 
that the installation is approved by the Fire 
official based on large-scale fire testing 
complying with Section 1207.1.5: 
3.1. NFPA 12, Standard on Carbon 
Dioxide Extinguishing Systems. 
3.2. NFPA 15, Standard for Water Spray 
Fixed Systems for Fire Protection. 
3.3. NFPA 750, Standard on Water Mist 
Fire Protection Systems. 
3.4. NFPA 2001, Standard on Clean 
Agent Fire-Extinguishing Systems. 
3.5. 3.5.NFPA 2010, Standard for Fixed 
Aerosol Fire-Extinguishing Systems. 
Exception: Fire suppression systems for lead-acid 
and nickel-cadmium battery systems at facilities 
under the exclusive control of communications 
utilities that operate at less than 50 VAC and 60 
VDC shall be provided where required by NFPA 
76. 
❖ 
Suppression systems are required for most 
types of ESS as addressed in Sections 1207.7 
through 1207.10 with the exception of rooftop 
and open parking garage installations open to 
the sky or open parking garages where full-
scale testing demonstrates that such protection 
is not required (see Section 1207.9.4). 
 
Section 1207.5.5.1 addresses battery systems 
that contain water-reactive materials where 
water cannot be used. 
 
This section provides three options. Item 1 
provides a prescriptive requirement for the use 
of automatic sprinkler systems with specific 
design criteria. Item 2 allows a differentiation 
from this criterion where demonstrated to 
perform during a large-scale fire test. Likewise, 
Item 
3 
allows 
alternative 
automatic 
suppression systems where performance is 
demonstrated through a large-scale fire test 
similar to that in Item 2. Essentially, Items 2 and 
3 must specifically pass UL 9540A. 
 
The 
telecommunications 
industry 
has 
continually stressed the need for the continuity 
of telephone service, and the ability to maintain 
this service is of prime importance. This service 
is a vital link between the community and the 
various life safety services, including fire, 
police and emergency medical services. The 
integrity of this communications service can be 
jeopardized not only by fire, but also by water, 
from 
whatever 
the 
source. 
The 
telecommunications industry utilizes other 
means to achieve an exceptional record of fire 
safety and reliability. The exception applies 
only to those spaces or areas that are used 
exclusively for telecommunications equipment. 
Historically, those spaces have a low incidence 
of fire events. Fires in telecommunications 
equipment are difficult to start and, if started, 
grow slowly, thus permitting early detection. 
Such fires are typically of the smoldering type, 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1028 
 
do not spread beyond the immediate area and 
generally self-extinguish. 
1207.5.5.1 
Water-reactive 
systems. 
Electrochemical ESS that utilize water-reactive 
materials shall be protected by an approved 
alternative automatic fire-extinguishing system in 
accordance with Section 904, where the installation 
is approved by the Fire official based on large-scale 
fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5. 
❖ 
Some battery systems utilize enough water-
reactive material in their construction that a 
traditional automatic sprinkler system is not an 
option. It should be noted that some battery 
systems such as lithium ion might be water 
reactive 
but 
can, 
in 
some 
cases, 
be 
suppressed with water effectively. This section 
requires that the system be based on full-scale 
fire and fault condition testing. Item 3 of Section 
1207.5.5 essentially allows the same concept 
based on largescale testing. 
1207.5.6 
Maximum 
enclosure 
size. 
Outdoor walk-in units housing ESS shall not exceed 
16 m × 2.4 m × 2.85 m) high, not including bolt-on 
HVAC and related equipment, as approved. 
Outdoor walk-in units exceeding these limitations 
shall be considered indoor installations and comply 
with the requirements in Section 1207.7. 
❖ 
A maximum enclosure size for walk-in units is 
established, corresponding to the largest ISO-
type containers used for these installations, to 
provide differentiation between a walk-in unit 
and an inside installation. 
1207.5.7 
Vegetation control. Areas within 
3 m on each side of outdoor ESS shall be cleared of 
combustible vegetation and other combustible 
growth. Single specimens of trees, shrubbery or 
cultivated ground cover such as green grass, ivy, 
succulents or similar plants used as ground cover 
shall be permitted to be exempt provided that they 
do not form a means of readily transmitting fire. 
❖ 
This provision reduces exposure from exterior 
fires on the ESS. Some ground cover is 
acceptable as long as such ground cover does 
not create a means of readily transmitting fire. 
Essentially, well-manicured grass poses little 
hazard; however, it must be maintained to 
reduce the risk of possible fire spread to the 
ESS. 
1207.5.8 
Means of egress separation. ESS 
located outdoors and in open parking garages shall 
be separated from any means of egress as required 
by the Fire official to ensure safe egress under fire 
conditions, but in no case less than 3 m. 
Exception: The Fire official is authorized to 
approve a reduced separation distance if large-scale 
fire testing complying with Section 1207.1.5 is 
provided that shows that a fire involving the ESS 
will not adversely impact occupant egress. 
❖ 
Separation from means of egress must be at 
least 3 m to ensure safe egress if a fire occurs 
in an ESS installation. The exception allows a 
lesser separation where large-scale testing 
demonstrates that a hazard does not exist. 
This provides flexibility for new technologies or 
situations where a 3 m separation is not 
possible. 
1207.6 Electrochemical ESS technology-specific 
protection. Electrochemical ESS installations shall 
comply with the requirements of this section in 
accordance with the applicable requirements of 
Table 1207.6. 
❖ 
This section simply introduces Table 1207.6, 
which provides technology-specific protection. 
These features include exhaust ventilation, 
explosion control, safety caps, spill control and 
neutralization, and thermal runaway. These 
features 
are 
based 
on 
each 
specific 
technology. For instance, lead-acid batteries 
contain liquid and will need spill control and 
neutralization whereas lithium-ion batteries do 
not as they do not have a liquid to spill. 
However, both technologies are subject to 
thermal runaway and need proper controls to 
address this in accordance with Section 
1207.6.5. 
 
Note a relieves communications utilities 
regulated by NFPA 76 from having to provide 
explosion control for lead-acid and nickel-
cadmium batteries given the capacity of the 
systems and the safety record of such facilities. 
 
Note 
b 
states 
that 
unless 
proper 
documentation is provided for other and 
perhaps newer technologies, all of these 
features are required. The default is the more 
restrictive application of the requirements 
without 
further 
information 
about 
the 
performance of these ESS technologies. 
 
Note c addresses the fact that spill control and 
neutralization 
for 
lead-acid 
and 
nickel-
cadmium batteries is required only where such 
batteries are vented and have the ability to 
create a spill. 
 
Note d acknowledges the fact that vented lead-
acid batteries are not likely to go into thermal 
runaway. 
 
Note e allows the use of a battery management 
system that was tested with the ESS to UL 
1973 for management of thermal runaway 
-

Table 1207.6.

pages.text:
CHAPTER 12—ENERGY SYSTEMS GENERAL COMMENTS 
 
  
SBC 801-CC-2024 
1029 
 
hazards. 
 
See the commentary to Section 1207.6. 
1207.6.1 
Exhaust 
ventilation. 
Where 
required by Table 1207.6 or elsewhere in this code, 
exhaust ventilation of rooms, areas and walk-in 
units containing electrochemical ESS shall be 
provided in accordance with SBC 501 and Section 
1207.6.1.1 or 1207.6.1.2. 
❖ 
This 
section 
addresses 
the 
necessary 
ventilation requirements based on either the 
conclusion of the hazard mitigation analysis or 
the specific battery technology requirements. 
Some batteries need more ventilation than 
others. 
 
As with any other building areas, ventilation of 
ESS areas must comply with the requirements 
of SBC 501. In many cases, basic ventilation 
based on human comfort will be sufficient to 
adequately ventilate battery spaces. The main 
concern with many types of batteries is the 
buildup of hydrogen within an enclosed space. 
This is not the case for lithium-ion batteries, but 
it is a concern for aqueous batteries such as 
lead-acid and nickel-cadmium battery types, 
which contain water. Hydrogen has a wide 
flammability range and is the lightest element 
on the Periodic Table of Elements, having an 
atomic number of 1. The gas mixture is 
potentially explosive when the amount of 
hydrogen in the air exceeds 4 percent by 
volume. There are two options provided for 
ventilation addressed in Section 1207.6.1.1 or 
1207.6.1.2. Sections 1207.6.1.2.1 through 
1207.6.1.2.3 apply to either approach taken for 
ventilation. Section 1207.6.1.2.4 is more 
specific to the method of ventilation addressed 
in Section 1207.6.1.2. 
1207.6.1.1 
Ventilation based on LFL. The 
exhaust ventilation system shall be designed to limit 
the maximum concentration of flammable gas to 25 
percent of the lower flammable limit (LFL) of the 
total volume of the room, area or walk-in unit 
during the worst-case event of simultaneous 
charging of batteries at the maximum charge rate, 
in accordance with nationally recognized standards. 
❖ 
This section provides a performance-based 
approach by requiring a ventilation system 
designed to limit the maximum concentration of 
flammable gas to 25 percent of the LFL of the 
total volume of the room, area or walk-in unit 
with the worst-case event of charging at the 
maximum charging rate. 
1207.6.1.2 
Ventilation based on exhaust 
rate. Mechanical exhaust ventilation shall be 
provided at a rate of not less than 5.1 L/sec/m2 of 
floor area of the room, area or walk-in unit. The 
ventilation shall be either continuous or shall be 
activated by a gas detection system in accordance 
with Section 1207.6.1.2.4. 
❖ 
This section simply prescribes a ventilation rate 
of not less than 0.0051 m3/(s × m2) of room 
area. This is an extremely conservative 
ventilation rate. This section does not require 
continuous ventilation where ventilation is 
activated by a gas detection system. 
1207.6.1.2.1 
Standby 
power. 
Mechanical 
exhaust ventilation shall be provided with a 
minimum of 2 hours of standby power in 
accordance with Section 1203.2.5. 
❖ 
Regardless 
of 
the 
approach 
used 
for 
ventilation, mechanical ventilation is required 
to be provided with 2 hours of standby power. 
This is very important with technologies subject 
to thermal runaway that can be continuously 
generating more flammable gases. 
1207.6.1.2.2 
Installation 
instructions. 
Required mechanical exhaust ventilation systems 
shall be installed in accordance with the 
manufacturer′s installation instructions and SBC 
501. 
❖ 
This section is merely ensuring that ventilation 
systems are installed as intended by the 
manufacturer and as required by SBC 501 for 
optimal performance. 
1207.6.1.2.3 
Supervision. Required mechanical 
exhaust ventilation systems shall be supervised by 
an approved central station, proprietary or remote 
station service in accordance with NFPA 72, or 
shall initiate an audible and visible signal at an 
approved constantly attended on-site location.  
❖ 
Where mechanical ventilation is utilized to 
keep the concentration of flammable gases, 
such as hydrogen, from reaching a flammable 
or explosive limit, some method of ensuring 
that the ventilation system is operational is 
necessary. This section provides two options, 
which include supervision or an audible and 
visual alarm at an approved location. A security 
desk that is operated 24 hours a day would be 
a reasonable location to meet the “constantly 
attended” criterion. 
 
It should be noted that according to the IEEE 
1635, ventilation rates can be very low. 
Hydrogen is extremely small and will pass 
through walls and ceilings. Maintaining a 
concentration 
of 
hydrogen 
in 
building 
construction is difficult. One example of a 
facility exploding due to hydrogen from 
batteries is the Rancho Cordova, California, 
-

SBC 801-CC-2024 
1029
